title: How to Work with Death Anxiety That Surfaces During Psychedelic Journeys
body:
1. Facing the Abyss: My First Dance with Death Anxiety on a Psychedelic Journey

There I was, lying back in the soft cushions of my favorite meditation chair, eyes closed, heart pounding, as the mushrooms started to reshape my world. It began innocuously enough with vibrant colors and a gentle hum in the back of my mind. Then, out of nowhere, an overwhelming wave of fear rose like a tidal surge - death itself. Not the abstract, philosophical sort I’d toyed with in books or quiet moments. No, this was raw, primal, bone-chilling. The feeling that everything I was, everything I loved, would vanish into nothingness.

It caught me off-guard. I wasn’t prepared for that kind of confrontation. Before that trip, I had read plenty about psychedelic journeys revealing hidden parts of ourselves, about mystical experiences and ego dissolution. But no manual had truly prepared me for how it feels to stare down the shadow of my own mortality, right there in the psychedelic depths. The fear was so intense I almost begged for it to stop, to skip ahead to the beautiful afterglow.

But it didn’t. Instead, I learned something crucial in that uncomfortable darkness: death anxiety during a psychedelic journey isn’t just fear. It’s a doorway. A doorway that can take you to deep healing if you don’t run from it. This realization came slowly, layered with many more trips and a lot of quiet reflection afterward. Over years, I saw that death anxiety is often the unspoken visitor in psychedelic therapy sessions, surfacing when the mind confronts its limits.

Somehow, it seemed like the mushrooms knew what I needed to face - my ultimate fear made manifest. And sitting with that fear, rather than fleeing it, became a catalyst for transformation. In that space, I found a strange kind of peace. A clarity that came from recognizing death not as the enemy but as a natural part of life’s unfolding.

If you’ve ever experienced this, you’re not alone. Whether it’s your first time or your fiftieth, death anxiety can knock hard on the door of your consciousness during psychedelic sessions, and how you respond to it matters deeply. So how do you work with this fear, not against it? How can you move through the terror and come out the other side changed, maybe even free?

This article is for anyone who's met death anxiety in the psychedelic space and wondered how to hold that space without breaking. I’ll draw on decades of research, stories from pioneers like Stanislav Grof and Rick Strassman, and practical ways to move through those deep waters safely. And if you’re curious about tools that can support your journey - like a gentle Reishi Mushroom Tincture that soothes the nervous system - stick around. Let’s start by understanding what death anxiety really is beneath the surface.

2. What Is Death Anxiety and Why Does It Surface During Psychedelic Experiences?

Death anxiety isn’t just a morbid fascination or a passing worry. It’s a deep-seated existential dread, a fear of the unknown that lies beyond life. For many, it’s tied to the self - the “I” that seems permanent suddenly feels fragile and temporary. When we take psychedelics, especially substances like psilocybin, DMT, or LSD, they can shake the foundation of the ego, blurring boundaries between self and other.

That dissolution of the ego - a state neuroscientist Robin Carhart-Harris calls “ego death” - is where death anxiety often emerges. Suddenly, the narrative we’ve held about ourselves, our place in the world, and our mortality dissolves, sometimes all at once. The mind is throwing off its usual defenses, revealing what lies beneath: the awareness that we will die, and possibly that “we” might cease to exist as we know it.

Scientifically, this makes sense. The default mode network (DMN), a brain system linked to self-referential thought and maintaining our sense of identity, quiets down during psychedelic states. Carhart-Harris’s studies at Imperial College London showed that this reduction in DMN activity corresponds with a loosening of rigid self-boundaries. Without the DMN’s filtering, the mind is exposed to raw experiences, including the reality of death.

But death anxiety is not just a cognitive realization. It’s also biological and emotional. The amygdala, the brain’s fear center, may light up as we anticipate the unknown. The body reacts - heart races, breath quickens - as if the threat is immediate. During a trip, this can feel like drowning inside your own mind.

Yet, not all death-related experiences on psychedelics are driven by fear. Many people report mystical or transcendental experiences where death feels more like a passage, a shedding of old skin. The difference lies partly in mindset and context. When a person approaches the experience with openness and support, that death anxiety can shift into acceptance and even awe.

This is where researchers like Stanislav Grof come in, who decades ago described “death-rebirth” phenomena during psychedelic sessions - a symbolic death followed by renewal. This cycle, Grof argued, mimics deep psychological healing processes. So, death anxiety is both a challenge and an opportunity during psychedelic journeys. It’s the mind’s way of exposing what we most need to reconcile.

Understanding that this fear is natural, expected, and a sign of deep inner work can help lessen its grip. It’s not a bug of psychedelics; it’s part of their function. They strip away the layers, bringing ultimate concerns to the surface so we can face them directly.

3. The Science Behind Facing Death Anxiety: What the Research Shows

In recent years, scientific inquiry into psychedelics and their therapeutic potential has expanded dramatically, offering some of the clearest looks yet at how these substances interact with death anxiety. One landmark study by Roland Griffiths and his team at Johns Hopkins is worth mentioning here. Their work with psilocybin showed that a single high-dose session often leads to what participants described as a “mystical experience” that profoundly changed their attitudes toward death.

In Griffiths’s 2016 study, terminally ill patients with life-threatening cancer were given psilocybin. Many reported less fear of death and improved mood for months afterward. The researchers concluded that such sessions can reduce death anxiety by facilitating experiences of unity, timelessness, and sacredness. This study provides a clinical backbone to the age-old tradition of psychedelics as tools for confronting mortality.

Carhart-Harris’s 2017 research added another layer by mapping brain activity during psychedelic states. They observed that with decreased DMN activity, there’s increased global brain connectivity, allowing new perspectives to emerge. This rewiring underlies the “ego death” phenomenon, which sometimes includes intense death anxiety but, paradoxically, can lead to lasting positive changes in how a person relates to their own existence.

Rick Strassman’s work with DMT, often called the “spirit molecule,” also offers insight. Strassman found that DMT trips frequently involved encounters with “otherworldly” realms and feelings of dying and being reborn. His observations hint that death anxiety during these experiences is part of a broader spiritual process, not just fear in the clinical sense.

Additionally, the body’s physiological response to death anxiety can be supported with natural remedies. Adaptogens like reishi mushroom have been used for centuries to calm nervous system hyperactivity. In fact, you can find a well-regarded Reishi Mushroom Tincture that many use to gently support their emotional balance during intense inner work.

Another angle comes from trauma research, which overlaps with death anxiety when you consider that many fears around death are rooted in unresolved trauma. Bessel van der Kolk’s seminal book The Body Keeps the Score dives into how trauma is stored in the body and mind, and most psychedelic therapy frameworks now recognize the need to integrate these insights when working through death anxiety as well.

All this evidence points to a complex interplay of brain chemistry, psychological defense, and spiritual opening. Death anxiety is not a failure of the mind but a sign it is undergoing deep transformation. Understanding this mixture of science and spirit can help us prepare better for these moments.

4. How to Work with Death Anxiety During a Psychedelic Journey: Practical Steps

When death anxiety surfaces mid-trip, it can feel like a tsunami threatening to pull you under. But there are ways to stay afloat and even use that wave to carry you somewhere new. Here’s what I’ve learned from my own journeys and those of countless others.

First, allow the fear to be there. Fighting it or trying to “escape” only tightens its grip. Instead, breathe into the sensation. Notice what your body’s doing - heart pounding, sweat breaking out, muscles tensing - and tell yourself it’s okay to feel this. The mind often panics because it has no safe container for the emotion. You become your own container by witnessing the fear non-judgmentally.

Next, remind yourself that this is the mushroom medicine doing its work. As Michael Pollan points out in How to Change Your Mind, psychedelics often bring up what’s buried deep inside. Death anxiety usually signals a core wound or unresolved fear - it’s not random. Naming it out loud if you have a sitter or guide can be powerful: “I’m afraid of dying.” Naming gives the fear less power.

If you’re alone, try grounding techniques. Focus on your breath and the rise and fall of your chest. Sometimes a gentle mantra like “I am safe” or “This will pass” helps. Others find it useful to visualize a “safe place” or to physically hold a comforting object. Some find an infusion of calming tea or something like reishi tincture soothing after the peak anxiety passes.

Having a trusted guide or sitter is ideal. They can remind you that what you’re experiencing is temporary and support you to explore the fear without judgment. If the anxiety feels overwhelming, ask them to guide you back to a more neutral or uplifting mental space by talking gently, playing soft music, or guiding you through a meditation.

Remember, this is a process, not a problem to be solved immediately. The fear may dissipate in waves, cresting and receding. Stay with it. Grof’s “death-rebirth” stages suggest that after the darkness comes a light. This light is often a new sense of meaning, love, or connection.

Finally, prepare before the trip. Reading about death anxiety can reduce surprise. Journaling about your fears beforehand or meditating on death as a natural process can set the stage for a gentler encounter during the journey. Proper preparation can mean the difference between panic and awe.

5. Common Challenges When Facing Death Anxiety on Psychedelics and How to Overcome Them

Not every trip handles death anxiety smoothly. I’ve seen several common struggles come up repeatedly, and knowing these ahead of time can be a lifesaver.

One challenge is the feeling of “losing control.” Psychedelics amplify surrender, but when fear is involved, surrender can feel like falling into a void without a net. The trick here is to reclaim some control by focusing on simple, repeatable actions - breathing, touching a piece of fabric, or moving your fingers. This doesn’t negate the experience but anchors you.

Another difficulty is feeling utterly alone. Death anxiety can make the self feel isolated in a vast, indifferent cosmos. If you’re with a guide or sitter, reaching out verbally or non-verbally can break that isolation. If alone, pre-selected music or a recorded meditation can remind you of human connection. Reading a passage from a trusted book, like van der Kolk’s The Body Keeps the Score, post-trip can also help rebuild that sense of safety.

Sometimes, the fear can spiral into panic attacks. This is tough because panic feeds the fear, creating a loop. The best antidote is grounding and slow, rhythmic breathing. Try 4 seconds in, hold 4, 4 out, hold 4. This slows the nervous system and signals safety. It’s also helpful to remind yourself that the panic is temporary and cannot hurt you physically.

Many also struggle with interpreting the death anxiety as a spiritual punishment or bad omen. This belief can intensify fear. Reframing the experience as a sign of healing - a messenger rather than a menace - is crucial. Reading accounts from respected figures like Stanislav Grof or Michael Pollan can help shift this perspective.

Lastly, integrating the experience afterward can be a hurdle. Sometimes, the memory of death anxiety is so intense people avoid thinking about it at all. This avoidance can stall healing. Journaling, therapy, or support groups can create a space to gently unpack the experience over time. The goal is not to erase the fear but to transform your relationship with it.

6. Bringing the Experience Home: Integrating Death Anxiety into Daily Life

After the trip ends and the psychedelic fades, the work doesn’t stop. The death anxiety you faced in the altered state often morphs into new insights about living. Integration is where the real medicine takes root.

I’ve found that one of the most helpful methods is mindfulness practice. Sitting with thoughts about death outside of the trip, observing them without judgment, helps dissolve lingering anxiety. The practice teaches you that death-related thoughts don’t have to control your mood or choices.

Another powerful tool is therapy, especially approaches that respect psychedelic experiences, like trauma-informed or somatic therapies. Bringing in ideas from Bessel van der Kolk’s work allows you to notice how fear of death might be stored in your body, helping release it over time.

Many people choose to develop small rituals around death and mortality. This might mean lighting a candle and reflecting, writing letters to loved ones, or creating art inspired by the experience. Rituals provide meaning and reinforce acceptance.

Community is essential too. Talking with others who have faced death anxiety in psychedelic settings normalizes the experience and reminds you that you’re not alone in the struggle or the growth. Psychedelic integration circles or trusted friends can make a big difference.

On a practical level, everyday habits that reduce baseline anxiety - like regular exercise, time in nature, proper sleep, and, for some, gentle supplements - help keep death anxiety from taking root in daily life. I recommend reishi mushroom tincture as a gentle ally for calming mind and body during times of heightened anxiety.

Long-term, confronting death anxiety during psychedelic journeys often leads to a softer relationship with mortality. Instead of fear, there can be curiosity. Instead of avoidance, a willingness to live more fully. This isn’t about becoming fearless but about learning to sit with fear and still find joy.

7. Sitting with the Mystery: A Final Reflection on Death, Psychedelics, and Life

Death anxiety is a harsh visitor, no doubt. But what if, instead of a visitor, it’s a teacher? Something that demands we stop, lower our shields, and truly see what it means to be alive? Psychedelics don’t merely remove the veil; they hold it up and ask us to look behind it without blinking.

I’ve come to think of death anxiety as a kind of cosmic call to presence. In the clear eyes of this fear, the usual distractions fall away. The petty worries, the endless plans, the small dramas - none of it holds much weight. What remains is raw existence, fragile and precious.

Echoing the writings of Grof and the experiences of so many, death anxiety can be a doorway to a deeper spirituality, a renewed sense of interconnectedness. It’s not about erasing the fear but learning to walk alongside it, even dance with it. This dance can change us profoundly.

So, when death anxiety surfaces on your journeys, consider it an invitation to meet the unknown with courage. It’s not a battle to win but a presence to hold. And in doing so, you might discover that life - fleeting, uncertain, unpredictable - is still stunningly beautiful.

Maybe that’s the quiet medicine of it all: in facing death, we find life. And in facing our fear of death, we find a deeper love for the moments we have. It’s not easy, but nothing worthwhile ever is.
